exercises.exerciseTypeName: A: Use the word given in capitals to form a word
exercises.title: A: Use the word given in capitals to form a word
exercises.instructions: Use the word given in capitals at the end of each line to form a word that fits in the gap in the same line.
exercises.studentInstruction: Use the word given in capitals at the end of each line to form a word that fits in the gap in the same line.
exercises.passage: Last summer, my friend Marco went hiking alone in the mountains. He was completely (1) ...... that some wild berries growing along the trail were dangerous. I cannot (2) ...... enough how important it is to research local plants before setting off! Marco ate several of the berries, which meant he urgently needed an (3) ...... of antidote at the nearest clinic, as they contained a highly (4) ...... substance. He began to feel incredibly (5) ...... , with severe cramps and dizziness setting in fast. A (6) ...... at the local hospital confirmed that, while no operation was needed, the case was serious. The (7) ...... Marco received involved close monitoring overnight. Before he was discharged, the doctor gave him a (8) ...... for anti-nausea tablets to take at home. Marco then discovered he was (9) ...... to one of the tablets and developed an itchy rash. Despite this setback, he made a full (10) ...... within ten days and vowed never to eat unknown berries again.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: B: Complete the second sentence using the word given
exercises.title: B: Complete the second sentence using the word given
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.

items.sentence:
Fortunately, Adrian doesn't need to take any medication. **no**
Fortunately, there ...... to take any medication.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is no necessity, so the correct answer is "is no need for Adrian".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "is no need for Adrian", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
The minister was forced to step down due to a serious illness. **led**
A serious illness ...... resignation.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is cause and result, so the correct answer is "led to the minister's".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "led to the minister's", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence:
There's no point in visiting the doctor for a minor headache. **worth**
It ...... the doctor for a minor headache.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not worth it, so the correct answer is "is not worth seeing".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "is not worth seeing", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:

I've ...... the physiotherapist at ten on Friday.

items.sentence:

I ...... on a strict diet!

items.sentence:

exercises.exerciseTypeName: C: Write a phrasal verb in the correct form to replace the words in bold
exercises.title: C: Write a phrasal verb in the correct form to replace the words in bold
exercises.instructions: Write a phrasal verb in the correct form to replace the words in bold.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write a phrasal verb in the correct form to replace the words in bold.

items.sentence: Rachel has started to suffer from a bad cold and can't make it to class today.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

wrongOptions.started to suffer from: "started to suffer from" is the highlighted error — use "come down with" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "started to suffer from", not elsewhere — replace it with "come down with" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.
explanationContent.question: Rachel has <mistake>started to suffer from</mistake> a bad cold and can't make it to class today.
explanationContent.fix: "started to suffer from" → "come down with" (apply the correction shown)
explanationContent.correctedSentence: Rachel has come down with a bad cold and can't make it to class today.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Rachel has come down with a bad cold and can't make it to class today.
